retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Abrasions are classified into 3 types based on clinical presentations and etiological factors. These include linear or scratch abrasions, grazed or brush abrasions, and patterned abrasions. Impact abrasions result from a swift blow and forceful blow. Patterned abrasions resulting from blows, collisions, and auto versus pedestrian accidents are examples of impact abrasions. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Etiology -- Grazed or Brush Abrasions
retrieved_docs.content: Grazed abrasions usually occur in multiple presentations and result from friction against a broad, rough surface. These are most commonly observed in incidents such as road traffic accidents and falls during sports activities. Grazed abrasions are caused by the body being dragged against a rough surface, leading to the scraping off of the epidermis. The depth of grazed injuries varies depending on the surface's irregularity and the body's speed. Extensive, grazed abrasions are sometimes referred to as "brush burns." [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: When polishing pastes with coarse or medium-sized particles are used, they may cause abrasion and damage to the tooth surface by scratching the enamel. A less polished appearance can be seen, increasing bacterial plaque retention. [7] Manual devices are time-consuming, and there is no control over the force applied, and they require more patient compliance. With engine-driven polishers, there is higher aerosol production, heat generation, and damage to the soft tissue if not properly attended.

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Etiology -- Patterned Abrasion
retrieved_docs.content: Patterned abrasions occur when perpendicular force is applied to the epidermis, resulting in an impression of the offending object. Depending on the duration of contact with the offending object, they can be further subclassified as pressure abrasion and impact abrasion. Pressure abrasions result from prolonged compression of the epidermis, and they require minimal force to occur. For example, a ligature mark produced using roughing material for hanging leaves an imprint of the material used. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Abrasions are among the most frequently encountered physical injuries. While many are superficial and can be managed at home with simple treatments, healthcare workers will likely encounter patients with abrasions requiring medical attention. It is important to note that abrasions often coexist with other injuries. The presence of abrasions should initiate a thorough investigation, and the abrasions should be managed with clinical and medicolegal considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Internal Tooth Whitening -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The most commonly used methods for bleaching nonvital teeth include the walking bleach technique, inside-outside bleaching, and in-office bleaching. [3] The walking and inside-outside bleaching techniques are reported to be similarly effective for internal bleaching. The walking bleach approach is preferred as the access cavity is sealed, thereby avoiding the need for trays at home. In contrast, the success of the inside-outside technique depends on patient compliance with using bleaching trays and cleaning the access cavity with an interdental brush between office visits. [4] The results of internal bleaching are more predictable in discolored teeth caused by trauma or necrosis than material-related tooth discoloration. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucositis -- Treatment / Management -- Mechanical Cleaning
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be encouraged to enhance their regular mechanical cleaning practices, including increasing the frequency of tooth brushing, using a soft toothbrush, replacing the toothbrush regularly, and cleaning interproximally. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: While a physical examination of abrasions is essential for treatment, the medicolegal examination holds even greater significance. Abrasions can be found on any body part but are commonly observed on exposed areas such as the head, neck, and extremities. When located on the neck or genitalia, abrasions may have a particular significance that requires further evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical -- Active or Inactive?
retrieved_docs.content: We can determine if a carious lesion is active or inactive according to several factors: [3] Location: is the lesion in a plaque stagnation area? Is it located along the gingival margin? Aspect: is it white or brown? Is it matte or shiny? Texture: is it rough or smooth? Integrity: is it cavitated or non-cavitated? Gingival bleeding on probing: is the gingiva bleeding on probing?

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical -- Active or Inactive?
retrieved_docs.content: A carious lesion is considered active when it shows more of these characteristics: it is whitish, matte, has a rough texture (when carefully touched with the tip of a blunt probe), has plaque stagnation, is cavitated, and if there is gingival bleeding. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Abrasions are frequently linked to physical trauma, often due to falls, impacts against hard or uneven surfaces, and the pressure exerted by objects. They are commonly observed with other forms of blunt force trauma, including contusions and lacerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth polishing is an oral prophylactic procedure that involves smoothing the tooth surface, making it glossy and lustrous, reducing plaque deposition, and maintaining periodontal health. Professionals typically use tooth polishing to refer to a dual cleaning and polishing procedure, even though the American Dental Hygienists Association clearly distinguishes between these terms. Cleaning is described as removing debris and extraneous matter from the teeth, and polishing makes the tooth surface smooth and lustrous.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For periodontal pocket reduction surgery, several local factors may reduce chances of regeneration, attachment gain, and pocket reduction, including root proximity, tipping or rotation of teeth, tooth mobility, thin mucosal tissue, the orientation of furcation entrances, enamel pearls, cervical enamel projections, denticles, ridges, root groves, and impacted teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The nature and context of abrasions are essential aspects that require adequate attention from clinicians to avoid potential negligence claims. Proper documentation, including photography of the injuries, should be preserved for a specified period.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation
retrieved_docs.content: Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficult if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the free gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (“heatless stone”) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.

retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is relevant in the epidemiological context of several disorders, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and تمزق الشبكية (retinal detachment). Recognizing that the allowed qualifier pertains to the epidemiology of these conditions highlights the importance of monitoring their occurrence, distribution, and risk factors within populations, thereby supporting public‑health planning, disease surveillance, and targeted preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2083
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to verlies; tanden door ongeval, indicating that unintended contact can result in tooth loss caused by an accident. The phrase “Due to” is also associated with verlies; tanden door ongeval, reinforcing the causal link between accidental physical contact and dental loss in traumatic situations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact is identified as the event that can cause loss of teeth, with the tooth loss directly linked to the accident itself. Injury (إصابة) is organized hierarchically, encompassing superficial injury; contusion (إصابة سطحية؛ كدمة) as a parent of the generic late‑effect category of unspecified injury (الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة غير محددة). This unspecified‑injury category includes numerous region‑specific late‑effect entities—upper extremity, neck and trunk, head, lower extremity—and broader chronic sequelae such as late effects of fracture of multiple bones, late effects of fracture of spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, late effects of burns, etching and freezing, and late effects of injury involving multiple body regions. The late‑effect hierarchy also contains the SMQ “المجموعة العدائية/العدوان” (SMQ), which itself comprises superficial injury; contusion and the behavior‑related concept سادية ممزوجة بتعذيب الذات (sadistic‑masochistic self‑harm), linking

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.
